Reflection: In today's class, we covered the standards that apply to sterile compounding in pharmacy. I learned that USP sets guidelines that pharmacies must follow when preparing sterile products. These standards cover things like cleanroom requirements and procedures for ensuring sterility. Understanding these requirements was important for me because it shifted how I think about compounding as a career path. Before today, I thought compounding was just about making personalized medications, but now I see it as a highly regulated, patient safety-critical practice. A single failure in the sterile environment can lead to contaminated products, patient infections, or even death--events that have actually occurred due to compounding pharmacy failures. This made me appreciate why sterile compounders must be extremely meticulous. I also realized that my initial assumption--that compounding was a relatively unregulated niche area of pharmacy--was completely wrong. In the future, I plan to apply these standards at my internship and beyond.

In today's class, I learned that USP Chapter <797> and USP Chapter <800> are the primary standards governing sterile compounding and hazardous drug handling in pharmacy settings. USP <797> establishes requirements for personnel training and competency assessment--including gloved fingertip sampling, media fill tests, and garbing competency evaluations--as well as environmental monitoring and beyond-use dating (BUDs) for sterile preparations based on compounding category and risk level. USP <800> addresses the safe handling of hazardous drugs as defined by the NIOSH Hazardous Drug List, requiring containment primary engineering controls (C-PECs), appropriate personal protective equipment, and defined procedures for spill management and waste disposal to protect healthcare workers from drug exposure. I also learned that ISO classifications define air quality in cleanroom environments: ISO 5 (critical zone), ISO 7 (buffer area), and ISO 8 (ante-area) each carry specific requirements for particulate counts and pressure differentials to maintain sterility.

These standards matter for my growth because they directly protect both patients and pharmacy staff. Before today, I understood that compounding had rules, but I had not appreciated how rigorously evidence-based and consequential those rules are. Contamination of sterile products has caused real patient harm--including the 2012 fungal meningitis outbreak linked to a compounding pharmacy in Massachusetts that resulted in over 750 illnesses and 64 deaths. Understanding that BUDs are assigned based on facility conditions and sterility testing--not arbitrary timelines--shifted how I think about precision in compounding practice.

Going forward, I will apply this learning at my internship by: (1) reviewing the site's standard operating procedures (SOPs) for sterile compounding and comparing them against current USP <797> and <800> requirements; (2) volunteering to observe environmental monitoring activities and competency assessments such as gloved fingertip sampling; and (3) completing all required garbing and media fill training before working independently in any cleanroom environment.

I learned about compounding standards today. There are rules that pharmacies need to follow when making medications, especially sterile ones. USP is one organization that sets these rules. Standards are important for patient safety. I think this will be useful when I work in a pharmacy someday.

Today's class provided a comprehensive overview of how the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) regulates controlled substances in pharmacy practice. I learned that drugs are classified into five federal schedules (Schedules I-V) based on medical utility and potential for abuse or physical or psychological dependence. Schedule I substances (e.g., heroin, LSD) have no accepted medical use and cannot be prescribed, while Schedules II-V are available by prescription with increasingly flexible requirements. Schedule II medications (e.g., oxycodone, fentanyl, amphetamines) require a new prescription each time--no refills are permitted--and must be ordered from suppliers using DEA Form 222 or the equivalent Controlled Substance Ordering System (CSOS). Schedules III-V allow up to five refills within six months. I also learned that all DEA-registered entities must conduct a biennial controlled substance inventory, maintain records for a minimum of two years, and use authorized pathways for disposal such as DEA-authorized collector sites or DEA Form 41 for reverse distribution. State prescription monitoring programs (PMPs) integrate with federal DEA oversight to help detect diversion patterns across prescribers and patients.

This is directly relevant to my growth as a future pharmacist because controlled substances represent one of the highest-risk areas in pharmacy practice. Dispensing errors, documentation failures, and diversion can have catastrophic consequences--patient harm, criminal prosecution, loss of DEA registration, and pharmacy closure. I had previously thought of controlled substance management primarily as administrative overhead--lots of forms and counts--but today's class reframed it as a critical public health and patient safety function. Given the ongoing opioid epidemic, I now understand more concretely how pharmacists serve as a key checkpoint in preventing diversion while still ensuring that patients with genuine clinical needs can access these medications.

To apply this in my practice, I will: (1) during my inpatient internship, review the facility's controlled substance policies and ask to shadow or assist with biennial inventory and daily count discrepancy resolution; (2) access my state's PMP system and practice running a patient's controlled substance history before filling a prescription to understand what patterns might trigger further review; and (3) ask my preceptor to walk me through the documentation and reporting process for a controlled substance discrepancy before I encounter one independently.

Today I learned about DEA regulations that apply to controlled substances in pharmacy. We discussed how drugs are placed into categories based on their abuse potential and whether they have an accepted medical use. I also learned about some of the recordkeeping and inventory requirements pharmacists must maintain for these medications. Learning about these regulations made me reflect on the role pharmacists play in the opioid crisis and in the broader tension between access and safety. It is easy to default to viewing every patient requesting a controlled substance with suspicion, but today's class challenged that assumption. Standards and regulations exist to create structured guardrails that protect vulnerable patients while preserving access for those who genuinely need these medications. I realized I had been thinking about controlled substance regulations primarily in adversarial terms--as a system for catching diversion--when in reality the framework is also designed to ensure that patients with legitimate pain or psychiatric needs can consistently receive medications that allow them to function and live with dignity. I will keep these regulations in mind as I advance in my pharmacy career.

Today we learned about rules and regulations in pharmacy. These regulations are important because they help keep patients safe and protect public health. I think following rules is an important part of being a pharmacist and something I will continue to work on in my career.

Today's lesson was about patient privacy and HIPAA. HIPAA is a law that protects patient health information in healthcare settings, including pharmacies. It applies to pharmacists and other healthcare providers who handle patient information. Patient privacy is something all pharmacists need to respect.

Today I learned that HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--is composed of two key rules especially relevant to pharmacy practice: the Privacy Rule, which governs the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI), and the Security Rule, which applies specifically to electronic PHI (ePHI). PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers--such as name, address, dates of service, prescription numbers, or biometric data--that relates to a patient's health status, care, or payment for care. I learned that pharmacists must apply the minimum necessary standard when sharing patient information, meaning only the information required for the specific task at hand should be disclosed. HIPAA permits disclosure of PHI without patient authorization for Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations (TPO), but other disclosures generally require a signed patient authorization. Violations carry tiered civil monetary penalties ranging from $100 to $50,000 per violation depending on culpability, as well as potential criminal liability for willful disclosures. This is important for my development as a pharmacist because patients must trust that their health information is handled with care. Without that trust, patients may withhold information that is critical to their safe care--putting themselves at risk. I will make sure to follow HIPAA standards when I work in pharmacy settings and be careful about how I share patient information.

In today's session, I learned how HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--applies to pharmacy practice in concrete, behavioral terms. The Privacy Rule protects Protected Health Information (PHI), defined as individually identifiable information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers (such as name, date of birth, address, or prescription number) that relates to a patient's health, treatment, or payment for care. The Security Rule extends these protections to electronic PHI (ePHI) and requires covered entities to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards. Pharmacists may share PHI without patient authorization for Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations (TPO), but must apply the minimum necessary standard--sharing only what is required for the specific purpose. I also learned that pharmacies must execute Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with third-party vendors that will have access to PHI, such as billing companies or pharmacy software vendors. Finally, HIPAA breach notification rules require covered entities to notify affected individuals, the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and--for breaches affecting more than 500 individuals in a state--relevant media outlets, all within 60 days of discovering the breach.

This resonated with me personally because early in my pharmacy experience, I observed a colleague reading out a patient's medication list at the front counter while other customers were nearby. At the time I felt uncomfortable but was unsure whether it was a HIPAA violation or simply poor practice. Today's class clarified that verbal disclosures of PHI in non-private settings where unauthorized individuals could overhear do constitute HIPAA violations. This reframed my understanding: HIPAA compliance is not primarily about securing computers or signing forms--it is an active behavioral standard embedded in every counter interaction, phone call, and counseling session.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) consistently moving to a private counseling area whenever discussing sensitive medication information with a patient, even under time pressure; (2) asking my preceptor at my internship to walk me through the site's breach investigation and notification protocol so I know what to do if I witness or accidentally cause a disclosure; and (3) reviewing the site's Business Associate Agreements to understand which vendors have access to patient data and for what purposes.

Today I learned about the Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA), federal legislation enacted in 2013 that established a national framework for tracking and tracing prescription drug products throughout the pharmaceutical supply chain. Key elements include the requirement for trading partners--manufacturers, wholesale distributors, repackagers, and dispensers including pharmacies--to exchange three specific transaction documents: Transaction Information (TI), Transaction History (TH), and Transaction Statement (TS), collectively referred to as T3 documentation. I also learned about drug product serialization: every prescription drug package must carry a standardized Drug Product Identifier (DPI) encoded in a 2D data matrix barcode containing the National Drug Code (NDC), lot number, expiration date, and a unique serial number. Pharmacies are required to verify product identifiers for saleable returns before reintroducing them into inventory, investigate products flagged as suspect using a defined process, and report illegitimate products to the FDA. The law also set a target for an Enhanced Drug Distribution Security (EDDS) system enabling fully interoperable electronic tracing at the individual package level, though implementation has faced industry-wide delays.

This content shifted my understanding of the pharmacist's role in ways I did not expect. I had not previously thought of a drug's arrival at the pharmacy shelf as the endpoint of a regulated, documented chain of custody. Nor had I considered that pharmacists are active participants in supply chain security rather than just clinical care providers. Drug counterfeiting, diversion, and contamination are real threats that can directly harm patients--and the DSCSA exists to close the gaps where these threats can enter the supply chain. Knowing that I may one day need to quarantine a suspect product, investigate a discrepancy, or reject a saleable return that fails verification expanded my understanding of pharmacist responsibility well beyond the dispensing counter.

I will apply this learning by: (1) asking my preceptor at my internship to explain the site's process for receiving pharmaceutical shipments and how T3 documentation is reviewed and stored; (2) reviewing the FDA's published guidance on suspect and illegitimate product investigation procedures so I understand the procedural steps before I encounter one; and (3) asking the pharmacy team whether their dispensing system supports 2D barcode scanning for DSCSA verification and what the manual fallback process is when the system is unavailable.

Today we discussed regulations that govern the safety and legitimacy of drug products as they move through the supply chain from manufacturers to pharmacies to patients. These standards help ensure that medications arriving at pharmacies can be traced back to their origin and verified as legitimate. This is important because it prevents counterfeit or diverted drugs from reaching patients, which could cause serious or life-threatening harm. At my internship, I will pay attention to how drug shipments are received and verified at my site.

Drug regulations are very important in pharmacy practice. I am looking forward to applying what I have learned about drug safety and medication standards to my internship experience. I will make sure to follow all rules and guidelines related to medications in my future practice.

Today's class focused on the regulatory authority I will personally answer to throughout my career: the State Board of Pharmacy. I learned that, while federal agencies (FDA, DEA, HHS) set drug-product and substance-level regulations, the practice of pharmacy itself is licensed and disciplined at the state level. Each state's Board of Pharmacy promulgates regulations on scope of practice, technician-to-pharmacist ratios, pharmacist-in-charge responsibilities, continuing education requirements (typically 15–30 CE hours per year, with required hours in patient safety, opioid management, and immunization), and disciplinary processes for license violations. We also reviewed the Multistate Pharmacy Licensure Compact and the difference between license reciprocity by score transfer (NAPLEX score transfer) and reciprocity by examination, which affects practical career mobility.

This learning mattered to me because I had been thinking of "pharmacy regulation" mostly in federal terms — DEA, FDA, HIPAA — and had not appreciated that the state board has the most direct day-to-day authority over how I practice and whether I keep my license. Realizing that the rules for things like remote verification, technician scope, and CE compliance vary by state changed how I think about taking a job out of state, and clarified that part of my professional development is learning my state board's regulations as carefully as I am learning federal ones.

I will apply this by (1) downloading and reading my state's Pharmacy Practice Act and accompanying regulations before I graduate, with particular attention to the technician scope, remote verification rules, and CE requirements; (2) signing up for my state board's email list so I receive regulatory updates and disciplinary actions, which I will read for the lessons-learned content; and (3) building a personal CE-tracking spreadsheet from year one of practice so I never end a renewal cycle short on hours or topic-required content.

Today we learned about the State Board of Pharmacy and its role in regulating pharmacy practice at the state level. State boards license pharmacists, set rules for technicians, and require continuing education. CE requirements vary by state. This is important because state boards are who I will deal with in my career. I will pay attention to my state's rules.
State boards regulate pharmacists. They have rules. There are CE requirements. I will follow them.

Today's session covered Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies (REMS) programs as the FDA's mechanism for ensuring that the benefits of certain high-risk drugs continue to outweigh their risks after approval. I learned that REMS are not optional safety suggestions — they are FDA-required conditions of approval, and dispensing a REMS drug outside the program is non-compliant for the pharmacy. REMS elements can include Medication Guides, Communication Plans, and Elements to Assure Safe Use (ETASU) such as required prescriber certification, mandatory patient enrollment, lab monitoring (e.g., absolute neutrophil count for clozapine), and certified-pharmacy registration. We also reviewed the move toward shared system REMS (e.g., the Transmucosal Immediate-Release Fentanyl REMS, the iPLEDGE REMS for isotretinoin) where multiple manufacturers participate in a unified program.

This shifted my picture of post-market drug regulation. I had thought of FDA approval as a single binary event. The REMS framework reframes it as a conditional approval where the manufacturer accepts ongoing risk-management obligations, and the pharmacist becomes one of the points where those obligations are operationalized. Personally, this clarified that REMS errors are not minor administrative slips — failing to verify clozapine ANC or filling an isotretinoin prescription without a current iPLEDGE confirmation is both unsafe for the patient and a regulatory violation for the pharmacy.

I will apply this by (1) asking my retail and inpatient preceptors to walk me through how their site handles three current REMS programs (likely clozapine, isotretinoin, and TIRF) so I see the actual workflow rather than the abstract rule; (2) maintaining a personal reference list of the active REMS programs relevant to my practice setting; and (3) committing, when I encounter a REMS-controlled prescription, to verify the REMS element rather than assume the prescriber's office has already done so — the pharmacy is a co-equal responsible party.

We discussed REMS programs today. REMS stands for Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies, and they are FDA-required programs for high-risk drugs like clozapine and isotretinoin. They include elements like patient enrollment, lab monitoring, and prescriber certification. This is important because pharmacies have to follow REMS to dispense the drug. I will be aware of REMS in my practice.
REMS programs are for risky drugs. The FDA requires them. I will follow them if I work with these drugs.

Today's class drew the distinction between 503A pharmacies (traditional patient-specific compounding) and 503B outsourcing facilities (office-stock compounding under cGMP), and the regulatory implications of each. I learned that 503A pharmacies compound on receipt of a prescription for an identified patient, are regulated primarily by state boards under USP <797> and <800>, and may not produce drugs that are essentially copies of commercially available products. 503B outsourcing facilities, created by the Drug Quality and Security Act after the 2012 NECC fungal meningitis outbreak, register with the FDA, follow current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP), and may produce sterile compounds in bulk for office stock without patient-specific prescriptions, subject to FDA inspection. We also reviewed the practical implications: a hospital sourcing decisions about which compounded products to bring in-house versus outsource is a real-money decision that turns on this distinction.

What this learning crystallized for me was why the 503A/503B framework exists at all — it is the regulatory response to a real, recent harm event (NECC) that killed 64 people. The distinction is not bureaucratic; it is the way the system tries to ensure that bulk sterile compounding carries manufacturer-grade oversight while preserving the ability of community and hospital pharmacies to compound for specific patients. I had previously thought of compounding regulation as monolithic; understanding the two-tier structure clarified the trade-offs that hospital pharmacy directors actually make.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the site uses 503B outsourcing for any sterile compounded products and, if so, how the supplier was vetted; (2) reading at least one FDA inspection report (Form 483) of a 503B facility to understand what cGMP findings actually look like; and (3) being able, by the end of my advanced rotations, to articulate coherently when 503A versus 503B sourcing is appropriate so I can contribute to that conversation as a new pharmacist.

Today we learned the difference between 503A pharmacies and 503B outsourcing facilities. 503A is traditional patient-specific compounding regulated mostly at the state level, while 503B facilities follow FDA cGMP and can compound in bulk for office stock. The 503B framework was created after the NECC outbreak. This is important to understand for hospital sourcing decisions.
There are different kinds of compounding pharmacies. Some are 503A and some are 503B. They are regulated differently.

Today's class covered the FDA drug approval pathway and post-market pharmacovigilance, with attention to the pharmacist's role at each stage. I learned the structure of the IND/NDA process — preclinical, Phase I (safety, small healthy-volunteer), Phase II (efficacy, dose-finding), Phase III (large randomized comparison), NDA submission and FDA review — and the difference between standard, priority, breakthrough, and accelerated approval designations. We also reviewed Phase IV / post-market surveillance: REMS, FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), MedWatch reports, manufacturer Periodic Safety Update Reports, and label updates triggered by real-world signals. The instructor walked us through several real cases (rofecoxib, rosiglitazone, troglitazone) where serious risks emerged only after widespread post-market use.

This was important because I had implicitly thought of FDA approval as the endpoint of drug evaluation. Today's class made clear that the pre-approval trials, while necessary, cannot detect rare adverse events, long-latency harms, or interactions that surface only at population scale. Pharmacovigilance is the actual safety net, and pharmacists submit a substantial portion of all MedWatch reports — meaning my future MedWatch submissions are not optional good citizenship but a structural part of how the FDA learns about drugs after approval.

I will apply this by (1) reading the FDA MedWatch reporting form before my first rotation so the form is not new to me when I encounter a reportable event; (2) asking each preceptor about the institution's process for routing suspected ADRs and committing to file at least one MedWatch report during my rotations under preceptor supervision; and (3) building a habit, when I see a new drug being prescribed, of looking up its approval pathway and any post-market label updates so I am thinking about its safety profile in context rather than as a fixed package insert.

Today's class covered the FDA drug approval process and how the FDA monitors drugs after approval. We talked about the phases of clinical trials and how post-market surveillance through MedWatch and FAERS is used to catch problems that did not show up in trials. This is important because pharmacists submit many MedWatch reports. I will be aware of post-market safety issues in my career.
The FDA approves drugs and watches them after approval. Pharmacists report problems. This is how drug safety works.
